sentences.text: David Jacobs.
J-A-C-O-B-S.
David.
D-A-V-I-D.
Yeah.
Jakubowicz.
J-A-K-U-O-B-W-I-C-Z.
Yeah.
J-A-K-U-B-O-W-I-C-Z.
No.
Dovid.
1918, the 28th of July.
In Tomaszow Mazowiecki.
Hits
Poland.
T-O-M-A-S-Z-O-W M-A-Z.
That spells Mazowiecki.
Right.
Towia.
He was a tailor.
And a nice person, in one word.
He was born nine kilometers from Tomaszow, that's called Ujazd.
Oh, he was about-- I think about 56, something like this.
It was very successful.
We had a lot of customers, as a matter of fact, German customers.
There had been a lot of German in Tomaszow, living there before the war.
And they were very good customers.
And we did work for them.
No, we have people what did work for us.
I would say about four people.
We had a very good relationship.
Because my father was orthodox.
And sometimes he looked away things.
He always liked to see the kids.
They're going with him to the synagogue, which did make him happy.
And later on, we were on our own.
Not always look away.
He didn't like to, but accordingly, the younger generation, they had their mind on their own.
They liked to go to a club, where is sporting.
And that's what it was.

That he knew that we went away to some- that on Saturday, which is a Jewish religion holy day, instead of going to the synagogue after lunch, we went away, as young kids, we tried to play with the other kids and go--
We went, we played soccer, we played ping pong.
And that's all.
Not really.
As long he didn't see it.
Hana Feiger.
Michlewicz.
No, no.
The only thing I know, she was about between five and six years younger.
When they start to get rid of every Jewish person.
In Tomaszow.
Like they send them out to Treblinka.
In 1940-- between 1942 and 1943.
She would be about-- between 48 and 50 years.
Yeah.
My mother, she was a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she always, the kids, the kids.
And she did look so much after us, like every mother.
I beg your pardon?
Yeah, very much so.
Also to my father.
The whole family was very close.
No.
We've been seven together.
Like four brothers-- sorry.
Two sisters and five brothers.
Nojech.
Jacobs.
Jakubowicz.
In 1905, November the first.
Something like this, November, I think so.
The oldest sister was Cluwa.
You know, her name was Cluwa.
Like here, she would be called Celine.
Jakubowicz also.
Yeah.
Boguchwal.
Right.
Right.
Naftula.
In 19-- 1915, I think, if I'm correct.
Yeah, it would be 1915, I think so.
It was Berju.
Berju.
Well, he was born in 1916. 1915, 1916-- it doesn't sound-- I know he was two years older than me.
So Naftula must have been born in 1914.
To Berju?
Me.
Chaskl.
He was born in 1920.
Was Chuma.
Is Chuma.
She is in Toronto too.
We were very, very close to each other, very much so.
It's a close family, very close.
We lived together, and we stayed together.
And then, when my older brother got married, we were very, very close.
Yeah.
Right.
No.
Right, yeah.
She had three boys and one girl.
One was Leizer.
One was Gutman.
And one was Moti.
And the girl's name I don't remember.
She was the youngest.
The little girl was about two years old.
And then the other ones were five, eight, and one was 13 already.
Boguchwal.
We have one, two, three, four, five rooms.
And then we a at separate room.
Because we had our own house, with tenants, too.
But not in our apartment, in separate apartments.
My brother, Chaskl.
Never, never.
Not very much.
I would say, I would say the beds.
Maybe there did stay something there, the books or whatever it is.
It wasn't like in Toronto.
My brother was a very-- the younger-- he was very, very good in school.
I remember he was three years a tailor.
And then he said, I want to be somebody and started to go to high school.
And he was a very, very good student, very ambitious.
Chaskl, my younger brother.
Oh, no question about it.
I remember he said, all right, the rent what that tenant is paying would be paid for in the school for you.
He had 36 tenants.
No, there were four buildings.
The first one was an apartment.
They did call in the old country like apartments.
But they were houses, let's say.
There were eight rooms in one apartment, meant like a room in a kitchen, like eight apartments.
Oh yeah, yeah.
Yes.
There used to live there my mother's brother in one apartment.
Michlewicz, Avrum Michlewicz.
Then my older sister lived also in our apartment.
And then my grandparents from my father's side, they became older, and my father brought them over and gave them an apartment.
And they lived also there.
After coming home from the shul, like from the synagogue- it was not a synagogue.
It was like-- in the old country they said a one room to describe like a shtiebel, that's what it was called.
Let's say a small, very small, synagogue.
And we came home.
And we ate-- I remember I used to go to the bakery next door.
And my mother made on Friday a cholent, if you know what that means, a cholent.
And we had to go there and bring it home.
If we wouldn't go on time, somebody else would have taken it.
No, that was the custom.
It had to stay overnight, a cholent.
Because the baker made the oven.
And in the old country, it wasn't like you had all the kind of conveniences that you have here.
Now you put it on an electric stove, and it has to stay over night.
But there it wasn't.
Yeah, yeah.
Because you couldn't turn off electricity, or you couldn't turn it on, the electricity.
Well, it was a custom like this.
No, I didn't have a bar mitzvah.
I had only what they called to the-- to [INAUDIBLE] and read my-- whatever I had to read.
That's right.
Yeah, but I didn't have at home like to make a supper or whatever it is.
Oh, yeah.
There was a kaddish, sure.
Yeah, everybody had a drink.
Whatever that was, I don't remember.
Maybe it was wine and some cookies.
This I remember.
In a way, in a way.
I didn't know it.
To be honest, I didn't know very much about it.
Well, Tomaszow was a very nice, beautiful city, well, a small town.
There were between 15,000 and 16,000 Jewish people living in Tomaszow.
And it was a very beautiful, young generation, very beautiful.
It's very-- it's so hard to describe when you talk about this.
We'd always been happy, very happy.
And we always, like I said, we went as young people.
Well, after I would say about 14, 15 years, and he met together.
We had some very nice organizations, like [INAUDIBLE] and Hagibor, all kinds of Zionist organizations.
And we spent a lot of time-- like I have some time after school, Saturday mostly.
And we met together, at night, sometimes until about 9, 10 o'clock.
And then we had to go home.
But the young boys and girls.
Everybody is saying this.
But I think we had the nicest young boys and girls.
That was just sport.
We played soccer, we played ping pong.
We had-- had it called the JTGS.
The most time I spend there.
And all the young boys and girls there too.
It's called-- in Polish it's called [POLISH].
The Jewish Organization Sport Fed-- that's what I did translate.
I did play everything.
I even played basketball.
The best, what I was very, very good, was ping-pong.
I played soccer, too.
Absolutely.
JTGS.
And we had our own soccer club.
Oh, we played a lot of teams.
We had one German team was called Victoria.
And then there was [INAUDIBLE] Then there was [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] was like to say that was a club like the NDP, was called [INAUDIBLE].
And then was Hakoah, and one was [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, very much so.
If they could knock your feet, they did it.
And if we won, they could fight after the game with us too.
Oh, a lot of times.
I recall that one time was played [INAUDIBLE] and from Lodz, a club called [INAUDIBLE].
And they did play for a higher-- like there was divided A, B, C, and to go in a higher category.
And at that time, the [INAUDIBLE] won.
And that was a very, very bad that time, the fighting, after the game.
